    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 632
    21→
    22→Erdős, Rubin, and Taylor asked whether $(a,b)$-choosability of a graph implies
    23→$(am,bm)$-choosability for every positive integer $m$. Dvořák, Hu, and Sereni disproved this
    24→by constructing a 4-choosable graph that is not $(8,2)$-choosable.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/632](https://www.erdosproblems.com/632)
    27→
    28→[ERT80] Erdős, P., Rubin, A.L., and Taylor, H., *Choosability in graphs*, Proceedings of the
    29→West Coast Conference on Combinatorics, Graph Theory and Computing, Congressus Numerantium 26
    30→(1980), 125-157.
    31→
    32→[DHS19] Dvořák, Z., Hu, X., and Sereni, J.-S., *A 4-choosable graph that is not
    33→(8:2)-choosable*, Advances in Combinatorics (2019).
    34→-/
    35→
    36→open SimpleGraph
    37→
    38→namespace Erdos632
    39→
    40→/-- A graph $G$ is $(a,b)$-choosable if for every assignment $L$ of a list of at least $a$
    41→colors to each vertex, there exists a choice of a subset $S(v) \subseteq L(v)$ of size $b$
    42→for each vertex $v$, such that the chosen subsets of adjacent vertices are disjoint. -/
    43→def IsABChoosable {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (a b : ℕ) : Prop :=
    44→  ∀ L : V → Finset ℕ, (∀ v, a ≤ (L v).card) →
    45→    ∃ S : V → Finset ℕ, (∀ v, S v ⊆ L v) ∧ (∀ v, (S v).card = b) ∧
    46→      (∀ u v, G.Adj u v → Disjoint (S u) (S v))
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős Problem 632 [ERT80] (disproved by Dvořák, Hu, and Sereni [DHS19]):
    50→If $G$ is $(a,b)$-choosable then $G$ is $(am, bm)$-choosable for every positive integer $m$.
    51→-/
    52→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    53→theorem erdos_632 : answer(False) ↔ ∀ (V : Type*) (G : SimpleGraph V) (a b m : ℕ),
    54→    1 ≤ m → IsABChoosable G a b → IsABChoosable G (a * m) (b * m) := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→/--
    58→Dvořák, Hu, and Sereni [DHS19] constructed a specific counterexample: a graph that is
    59→4-choosable (i.e., (4,1)-choosable) but not (8,2)-choosable, disproving the conjecture
    60→of Erdős, Rubin, and Taylor [ERT80] for the case $m = 2$.
    61→-/
    62→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    63→theorem erdos_632_counterexample : answer(True) ↔
    64→    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    65→      IsABChoosable G 4 1 ∧ ¬IsABChoosable G 8 2 := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→end Erdos632
    69→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 633
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/633](https://www.erdosproblems.com/633)
    23→
    24→Classify those triangles which can only be cut into a square number of congruent
    25→triangles. **$25 reward.**
    26→
    27→Any triangle can be cut into $n^2$ congruent triangles for any $n \geq 1$ [So09].
    28→Soifer [So09c] reported Erdős's question and proved that there exists at least one
    29→triangle (e.g. one with sides $\sqrt{2}$, $\sqrt{3}$, $\sqrt{4}$) which can only be cut
    30→into a square number of congruent triangles. In fact, Soifer [So09b] proved that any
    31→triangle for which the angles and sides are both integrally independent has this property.
    32→The full classification remains open.
    33→
    34→See also Problem 634 for related results on which numbers $n$ admit a congruent dissection
    35→of an equilateral triangle.
    36→
    37→[So09] Soifer, A., *How Does One Cut a Triangle? I*, The Mathematical Coloring Book,
    38→Springer, 2009, pp. 15–23.
    39→[So09b] Soifer, A., *How Does One Cut a Triangle? II*, The Mathematical Coloring Book,
    40→Springer, 2009, pp. 37–39.
    41→[So09c] Soifer, A., *Is There Anything Beyond the Solution?*, The Mathematical Coloring
    42→Book, Springer, 2009, pp. 47–50.
    43→-/
    44→
    45→namespace Erdos633
    46→
    47→/-- Squared Euclidean distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    48→noncomputable def sqDist (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    49→  (p.1 - q.1) ^ 2 + (p.2 - q.2) ^ 2
    50→
    51→/-- Multiset of squared side lengths of a triangle.
    52→Two triangles are congruent (by SSS) iff these multisets agree. -/
    53→noncomputable def sideLengthsSq (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Multiset ℝ :=
    54→  ↑[sqDist A B, sqDist B C, sqDist A C]
    55→
    56→/-- A triangle is non-degenerate (vertices not collinear). -/
    57→def NonDegenerate (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    58→  (B.1 - A.1) * (C.2 - A.2) - (C.1 - A.1) * (B.2 - A.2) ≠ 0
    59→
    60→/-- The closed triangular region: convex hull of vertices $A$, $B$, $C$.
    61→A point $p$ lies in the triangle iff $p = \alpha A + \beta B + \gamma C$ for some
    62→$\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$. -/
    63→def triangleRegion (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    64→  {p | ∃ (α β γ : ℝ), 0 ≤ α ∧ 0 ≤ β ∧ 0 ≤ γ ∧ α + β + γ = 1 ∧
    65→    p.1 = α * A.1 + β * B.1 + γ * C.1 ∧
    66→    p.2 = α * A.2 + β * B.2 + γ * C.2}
    67→
    68→/-- The open interior of a triangular region: strictly positive
    69→barycentric coordinates. -/
    70→def triangleInterior (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    71→  {p | ∃ (α β γ : ℝ), 0 < α ∧ 0 < β ∧ 0 < γ ∧ α + β + γ = 1 ∧
    72→    p.1 = α * A.1 + β * B.1 + γ * C.1 ∧
    73→    p.2 = α * A.2 + β * B.2 + γ * C.2}
    74→
    75→/-- Triangle $(A, B, C)$ can be dissected into exactly $n$ pairwise-congruent
    76→non-degenerate triangles that tile it: each is contained in the original,
    77→their interiors are pairwise disjoint, and their union covers the original. -/
    78→def CanDissectInto (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    79→  ∃ (V : Fin n → (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ)),
    80→    -- All sub-triangles are non-degenerate
    81→    (∀ i, NonDegenerate (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2) ∧
    82→    -- All sub-triangles are mutually congruent (same squared side lengths)
    83→    (∀ i j, sideLengthsSq (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 =
    84→            sideLengthsSq (V j).1 (V j).2.1 (V j).2.2) ∧
    85→    -- Each sub-triangle is contained in the original
    86→    (∀ i, triangleRegion (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 ⊆
    87→           triangleRegion A B C) ∧
    88→    -- Pairwise disjoint interiors
    89→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j →
    90→      triangleInterior (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 ∩
    91→      triangleInterior (V j).1 (V j).2.1 (V j).2.2 = ∅) ∧
    92→    -- Union covers the original triangle
    93→    (∀ p, p ∈ triangleRegion A B C →
    94→      ∃ i, p ∈ triangleRegion (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2)
    95→
    96→/-- Euclidean distance (side length) between two points. -/
    97→noncomputable def sideLength (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    98→  Real.sqrt (sqDist p q)
    99→
   100→/-- The sides of a triangle are integrally independent: no nontrivial integer
   101→linear combination of the three side lengths equals zero. -/
   102→def IntIndepSides (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
   103→  ∀ (a₁ a₂ a₃ : ℤ),
   104→    ↑a₁ * sideLength B C + ↑a₂ * sideLength A C +
   105→      ↑a₃ * sideLength A B = 0 →
   106→    a₁ = 0 ∧ a₂ = 0 ∧ a₃ = 0
   107→
   108→/-- The angle at vertex $P$ in triangle $PQR$, via the dot-product formula:
   109→$\arccos\bigl(\frac{\vec{PQ} \cdot \vec{PR}}{|PQ| \cdot |PR|}\bigr)$.
   110→Returns a value in $[0, \pi]$. -/
   111→noncomputable def angle (P Q R : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
   112→  Real.arccos (((Q.1 - P.1) * (R.1 - P.1) + (Q.2 - P.2) * (R.2 - P.2)) /
   113→    (sideLength P Q * sideLength P R))
   114→
   115→/-- The angles of a triangle are integrally independent: the only integer
   116→linear combination of the angles that equals an integer multiple of $\pi$
   117→is the trivial one (all coefficients equal). This accounts for the
   118→constraint $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi$. -/
   119→def IntIndepAngles (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
   120→  ∀ (a₁ a₂ a₃ k : ℤ),
   121→    ↑a₁ * angle A B C + ↑a₂ * angle B A C +
   122→      ↑a₃ * angle C A B = ↑k * Real.pi →
   123→    a₁ = a₂ ∧ a₂ = a₃
   124→
   125→/--
   126→Every non-degenerate triangle can be dissected into $n^2$ congruent triangles
   127→for any positive integer $n$.
   128→-/
   129→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   130→theorem erdos_633.variants.square_dissection
   131→    (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (hnd : NonDegenerate A B C)
   132→    (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) :
   133→    CanDissectInto A B C (n ^ 2) := by
   134→  sorry
   135→
   136→/--
   137→There exists a non-degenerate triangle which can be dissected into $n$
   138→congruent triangles only when $n$ is a perfect square. [So09c]
   139→-/
   140→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   141→theorem erdos_633.variants.existence :
   142→    ∃ A B C : ℝ × ℝ, NonDegenerate A B C ∧
   143→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto A B C n → ∃ m : ℕ, n = m ^ 2 := by
   144→  sorry
   145→
   146→/--
   147→If a non-degenerate triangle has integrally independent sides and angles,
   148→then it can only be dissected into a perfect square number of congruent
   149→triangles. [So09b]
   150→-/
   151→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   152→theorem erdos_633.variants.soifer
   153→    (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (hnd : NonDegenerate A B C)
   154→    (hsides : IntIndepSides A B C) (hangles : IntIndepAngles A B C) :
   155→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto A B C n → ∃ m : ℕ, n = m ^ 2 := by
   156→  sorry
   157→
   158→/--
   159→Classify those non-degenerate triangles that can only be cut into a perfect
   160→square number of congruent triangles. The full classification remains open.
   161→-/
   162→@[category research open, AMS 51]
   163→theorem erdos_633 :
   164→    {T : (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) |
   165→      NonDegenerate T.1 T.2.1 T.2.2 ∧
   166→      ∀ n, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto T.1 T.2.1 T.2.2 n → ∃ m, n = m ^ 2} =
   167→    answer(sorry) := by sorry
   168→
   169→end Erdos633
   170→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 634
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/634](https://www.erdosproblems.com/634)
    23→
    24→Find all $n$ such that there is at least one triangle which can be cut into $n$
    25→congruent triangles.
    26→
    27→[So09c] Soifer, A., *Is There Anything Beyond the Solution?*, The Mathematical Coloring
    28→Book, Springer, 2009, pp. 47–50.
    29→[SWW91] Snover, S., Waiveris, C., Williams, J., *Rep-tiling for triangles*,
    30→Discrete Mathematics (1991), 193–200.
    31→[Zh25] Zhang, Y., *Tiling Triangles With 2π/3 Angles*, arXiv:2512.22696, 2025.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos634
    37→
    38→/-- Multiset of side lengths of a triangle with vertices `T : Fin 3 → ℝ²`.
    39→    Two triangles are congruent (by SSS) iff these multisets agree. -/
    40→noncomputable def sideLengths (T : Fin 3 → ℝ²) : Multiset ℝ :=
    41→  ↑[dist (T 0) (T 1), dist (T 1) (T 2), dist (T 0) (T 2)]
    42→
    43→/-- Triangle `T` can be dissected into exactly `n` pairwise-congruent
    44→    non-degenerate triangles that tile it: each piece has affinely independent
    45→    vertices, all pieces are congruent, each is contained in the original,
    46→    their interiors are pairwise disjoint, and their union covers the original. -/
    47→def CanDissectInto (T : Fin 3 → ℝ²) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    48→  ∃ (V : Fin n → Fin 3 → ℝ²),
    49→    (∀ i, AffineIndependent ℝ (V i)) ∧
    50→    (∀ i j, sideLengths (V i) = sideLengths (V j)) ∧
    51→    (∀ i, convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i)) ⊆ convexHull ℝ (Set.range T)) ∧
    52→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j →
    53→      interior (convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i))) ∩
    54→      interior (convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V j))) = ∅) ∧
    55→    (∀ p, p ∈ convexHull ℝ (Set.range T) →
    56→      ∃ i, p ∈ convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i)))
    57→
    58→/-- There exists a triangle that can be dissected into `n` congruent triangles. -/
    59→def HasCongruentDissection (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    60→  ∃ T : Fin 3 → ℝ², AffineIndependent ℝ T ∧ CanDissectInto T n
    61→
    62→/-- Two triangles are similar if their side length multisets are proportional. -/
    63→noncomputable def AreSimilar (T₁ T₂ : Fin 3 → ℝ²) : Prop :=
    64→  ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ sideLengths T₁ = (sideLengths T₂).map (c * ·)
    65→
    66→/-- Triangle `T` can be dissected into `n` pairwise-similar non-degenerate triangles. -/
    67→def CanDissectIntoSimilar (T : Fin 3 → ℝ²) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    68→  ∃ (V : Fin n → Fin 3 → ℝ²),
    69→    (∀ i, AffineIndependent ℝ (V i)) ∧
    70→    (∀ i j, AreSimilar (V i) (V j)) ∧
    71→    (∀ i, convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i)) ⊆ convexHull ℝ (Set.range T)) ∧
    72→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j →
    73→      interior (convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i))) ∩
    74→      interior (convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V j))) = ∅) ∧
    75→    (∀ p, p ∈ convexHull ℝ (Set.range T) →
    76→      ∃ i, p ∈ convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i)))
    77→
    78→/-- Triangle `T` can be dissected into `n` non-degenerate triangles each similar to `T`. -/
    79→def CanDissectIntoSelfSimilar (T : Fin 3 → ℝ²) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    80→  ∃ (V : Fin n → Fin 3 → ℝ²),
    81→    (∀ i, AffineIndependent ℝ (V i)) ∧
    82→    (∀ i, AreSimilar (V i) T) ∧
    83→    (∀ i, convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i)) ⊆ convexHull ℝ (Set.range T)) ∧
    84→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j →
    85→      interior (convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i))) ∩
    86→      interior (convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V j))) = ∅) ∧
    87→    (∀ p, p ∈ convexHull ℝ (Set.range T) →
    88→      ∃ i, p ∈ convexHull ℝ (Set.range (V i)))
    89→
    90→/-- There exists a triangle that can be dissected into `n` triangles similar to itself. -/
    91→def HasSelfSimilarDissection (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    92→  ∃ T : Fin 3 → ℝ², AffineIndependent ℝ T ∧ CanDissectIntoSelfSimilar T n
    93→
    94→/--
    95→Erdős Problem 634: It is conjectured that no prime $p \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$
    96→has the property that some triangle can be cut into $p$ congruent triangles.
    97→-/
    98→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    99→theorem erdos_634 :
   100→    ∀ p : ℕ, Nat.Prime p → p % 4 = 3 → ¬ HasCongruentDissection p := by
   101→  sorry
   102→
   103→/--
   104→Every perfect square $n^2$ (with $n \ge 1$) has a congruent dissection:
   105→any triangle can be cut into $n^2$ congruent copies. (Folklore)
   106→-/
   107→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   108→theorem erdos_634.variants.squares (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) :
   109→    HasCongruentDissection (n ^ 2) := by
   110→  sorry
   111→
   112→/--
   113→For any positive integer $n$, the number $2n^2$ has a congruent dissection. [So09c]
   114→-/
   115→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   116→theorem erdos_634.variants.two_squares (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) :
   117→    HasCongruentDissection (2 * n ^ 2) := by
   118→  sorry
   119→
   120→/--
   121→For any positive integer $n$, the number $3n^2$ has a congruent dissection. [So09c]
   122→-/
   123→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   124→theorem erdos_634.variants.three_squares (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) :
   125→    HasCongruentDissection (3 * n ^ 2) := by
   126→  sorry
   127→
   128→/--
   129→For any positive integer $n$, the number $6n^2$ has a congruent dissection. [So09c]
   130→-/
   131→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   132→theorem erdos_634.variants.six_squares (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) :
   133→    HasCongruentDissection (6 * n ^ 2) := by
   134→  sorry
   135→
   136→/--
   137→For any positive integers $n$ and $m$, the number $n^2 + m^2$ has a congruent
   138→dissection. [So09c]
   139→-/
   140→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   141→theorem erdos_634.variants.sum_of_squares (n m : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) (hm : 1 ≤ m) :
   142→    HasCongruentDissection (n ^ 2 + m ^ 2) := by
   143→  sorry
   144→
   145→/--
   146→There is no triangle that can be cut into exactly $7$ congruent triangles. (Beeson)
   147→-/
   148→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   149→theorem erdos_634.variants.not_seven : ¬ HasCongruentDissection 7 := by
   150→  sorry
   151→
   152→/--
   153→There is no triangle that can be cut into exactly $11$ congruent triangles. (Beeson)
   154→-/
   155→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   156→theorem erdos_634.variants.not_eleven : ¬ HasCongruentDissection 11 := by
   157→  sorry
   158→
   159→/--
   160→Zhang [Zh25] proved that for integers $a \ge b \ge 1$, if
   161→$n \ge 3\lceil(a^2 + b^2 + ab - a - b)/(ab)\rceil$, then $n^2 ab$ has a
   162→congruent dissection.
   163→-/
   164→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   165→theorem erdos_634.variants.zhang (a b n : ℕ) (ha : 1 ≤ a) (hb : 1 ≤ b) (hab : b ≤ a)
   166→    (hn : 3 * ⌈((a : ℚ) ^ 2 + b ^ 2 + a * b - a - b) / (a * b)⌉₊ ≤ n) :
   167→    HasCongruentDissection (n ^ 2 * a * b) := by
   168→  sorry
   169→
   170→/--
   171→Every triangle can be cut into $N$ similar (not necessarily congruent) triangles
   172→for all $N \ne 2, 3, 5$. [So09c]
   173→-/
   174→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   175→theorem erdos_634.variants.similar (T : Fin 3 → ℝ²) (hT : AffineIndependent ℝ T)
   176→    (n : ℕ) (hn₁ : 1 ≤ n) (hn₂ : n ≠ 2) (hn₃ : n ≠ 3) (hn₅ : n ≠ 5) :
   177→    CanDissectIntoSimilar T n := by
   178→  sorry
   179→
   180→/--
   181→If the smaller triangles must be similar to the original triangle, the possible
   182→values of $n$ are exactly those of the form $k^2$, $k^2 + m^2$, or $3k^2$
   183→for positive integers $k, m$. [SWW91]
   184→-/
   185→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   186→theorem erdos_634.variants.self_similar_characterization (n : ℕ) :
   187→    HasSelfSimilarDissection n ↔
   188→    (∃ k : ℕ, 1 ≤ k ∧ n = k ^ 2) ∨
   189→    (∃ k m : ℕ, 1 ≤ k ∧ 1 ≤ m ∧ n = k ^ 2 + m ^ 2) ∨
   190→    (∃ k : ℕ, 1 ≤ k ∧ n = 3 * k ^ 2) := by
   191→  sorry
   192→
   193→/--
   194→The case $n = 19$ is the smallest open case: it is unknown whether any triangle
   195→can be cut into exactly $19$ congruent triangles. Since $19 \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$
   196→is prime, the main conjecture predicts this is impossible.
   197→-/
   198→@[category research open, AMS 52]
   199→theorem erdos_634.variants.not_nineteen : ¬ HasCongruentDissection 19 := by
   200→  sorry
   201→
   202→end Erdos634
   203→
